bins.name: Fast-track and automated processing
bins.desc: Amazon Energy recommends Order No. 2023-style mechanisms to prioritize ready projects and a fast-track for high-readiness large loads. It urges the Commission to incentivize or require transmission providers to use advanced grid modeling and AI-driven analysis to speed feasibility and system impact studies, while stressing that automation supports rather than replaces engineering judgment.
quotes: Amazon Energy recommends that any rule adopted by the Commission governing the interconnection of large loads include mechanisms like those in Order No. 2023 to prioritize projects that are ready for interconnection.

Amazon Energy encourages the Commission to include in any rule a "fast track" or priority processing track for large load projects that can demonstrate a high degree of readiness (e.g., projects that have (i) committed customers, (ii) secured all necessary permits, (iii) site control, (iv) secured financing or corporate commitments, and/or (v) an executed service agreement).
Amazon Energy encourages the Commission to leverage technology and process innovations to automate and accelerate large load interconnections.

The Commission should incentivize or require transmission providers to deploy modern software tools, such as advanced grid modeling and AI-driven analysis, that can conduct feasibility and system impact studies more expeditiously and efficiently.

Amazon Energy emphasizes that greater automation is not a substitute for sound engineering judgment, but a way to expedite engineers' and planners' work by handling administratively burdensome tasks and analyses in hours or days rather than weeks or months.

bins.name: Readiness criteria for eligibility
bins.desc: Amazon Energy proposes concrete readiness criteria for fast-track qualification and for deterring speculative requests, such as committed customers, secured permits, site control, secured financing or corporate commitments, and executed service or construction agreements.
quotes: Amazon Energy encourages the Commission to include in any rule a "fast track" or priority processing track for large load projects that can demonstrate a high degree of readiness (e.g., projects that have (i) committed customers, (ii) secured all necessary permits, (iii) site control, (iv) secured financing or corporate commitments, and/or (v) an executed service agreement).

requiring large load interconnection customers to make upfront study deposits, demonstrate a degree of project readiness (e.g., site control or an executed facilities construction contract), and face withdrawal penalties if they exit after certain milestones will discourage non-viable or speculative large load requests.

bins.name: Study deposits and withdrawal penalties
bins.desc: Amazon Energy endorses applying generator-style standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to large loads. It argues these measures deter non-viable and speculative requests and reduce cascading restudies.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports the proposal in the ANOPR that, "like generating facilities, load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties,"

requiring large load interconnection customers to make upfront study deposits, demonstrate a degree of project readiness (e.g., site control or an executed facilities construction contract), and face withdrawal penalties if they exit after certain milestones will discourage non-viable or speculative large load requests.

bins.name: Hybrid facility interconnection rights
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports pathways for loads and generators to interconnect and to study, model, and operate with some interrelationship at a shared point. It sees coordinated hybrid interconnection as a benefit to ratepayers and the bulk electric system that reduces administration for utilities, RTOs, and ISOs.
quotes: Amazon Energy also recognizes there may be benefits for all ratepayers to interconnecting large loads directly to the bulk electric system in coordination with the interconnection of generation facilities.

creating viable pathways to allow large load customers and generators to interconnect to the bulk electric system, and to study, model, and operate with some form of interrelationship would yield benefits for the bulk electric system as a whole, including reduced administration for utilities, RTOs/ISOs, and investors.

bins.name: Standardized milestones within each ISO or RTO
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports standardized study procedures, milestones, and readiness requirements for large loads, particularly within a common ISO or RTO. It argues standardization improves processing efficiency, transmission planning, and load-forecasting accuracy.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports the proposal in the ANOPR that, "like generating facilities, load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties,"

Amazon Energy urges the Commission to consider imposing similar standardization and requirements here to ensure the timely and efficient processing of large load interconnection requests, particularly for interconnections within the same ISO/RTO.

standardizing milestones and requirements for large load interconnections within a common ISO/RTO will lead to better transmission planning outcomes, and in turn, result in improved accuracy in load forecasting.

bins.name: Lower costs through better project quality
bins.desc: Amazon Energy contends that stricter entry criteria, standardized milestones, and enhanced reporting will produce market efficiencies, shorter development timelines, and lower costs for consumers.
quotes: Improving the quality of large load interconnection requests through stricter criteria and milestones and enhanced visibility into the processing of large load interconnections through standardized reporting will result in market efficiencies, shorter development timelines, and lower costs for consumers.

bins.name: Co-location of load with generation
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports co-location of large loads at generating facilities and coordinated load and generation interconnection to the bulk electric system. It urges the Commission to expeditiously issue guidance in Docket No. AD24-11-000 on the viability of co-location.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports co-location of large load projects.

Amazon Energy also recognizes there may be benefits for all ratepayers to interconnecting large loads directly to the bulk electric system in coordination with the interconnection of generation facilities.

creating viable pathways to allow large load customers and generators to interconnect to the bulk electric system, and to study, model, and operate with some form of interrelationship would yield benefits for the bulk electric system as a whole, including reduced administration for utilities, RTOs/ISOs, and investors.
Amazon Energy supports solutions that provide the most flexibility to meet the needs of customers and businesses, which can include co-location.

Amazon Energy respectfully encourages the Commission to expeditiously issue an order in the pending proceeding in Docket No. AD24-11-000, which would provide the industry with much needed guidance as to the viability of large loads co-locating at generating facilities going forward.

bins.name: Flexibility and customer optionality
bins.desc: Amazon Energy favors solutions that give customers and businesses the most flexibility in how large loads interconnect, including co-location among the available options.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports solutions that provide the most flexibility to meet the needs of customers and businesses, which can include co-location.

bins.name: Support for timely interconnection reform
bins.desc: Amazon Energy broadly supports reforms that, done correctly, connect new AI and advanced manufacturing demand quickly while strengthening grid reliability and affordability for all customers.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports reforms that, done right, will help the United States connect new AI and advanced manufacturing investment quickly, while strengthening grid reliability and affordability for all customers.

bins.name: Prospective application and grandfathering
bins.desc: Amazon Energy urges that any new rules apply prospectively and not to large load requests already in progress under existing procedures. It argues developers invested in reliance on current rules and that retroactive requirements could upend project economics, cause delay, or force withdrawals, so reforms should include grandfathering or transition mechanisms.
quotes: Amazon Energy respectfully urges the Commission to apply any new rules or policy changes adopted in this proceeding prospectively, and not to large load interconnection requests currently in progress under existing interconnection procedures.

Large load developers have expended significant time and resources developing new large load projects (e.g., completing feasibility and system impact studies, securing sites, permits, and long-lead equipment, and executing definitive agreements) and made substantial investments in reliance on existing interconnection rules and procedures.

Abruptly subjecting these in-progress projects to new requirements or study processes could upend their economics, cause substantial delay, or even force withdrawals, thereby undermining the goals of timely and orderly interconnection that the ANOPR seeks to advance.

bins.name: Aggregated reporting on interconnection outcomes
bins.desc: Amazon Energy recommends that transmission providers report non-confidential, aggregated data on large load interconnection outcomes, including withdrawal rates, success rates, and processing times, to support more accurate planning and better resource-adequacy and reliability decisions.
quotes: Amazon Energy recommends that the Commission direct transmission providers to report non-confidential, aggregated data on the processing of large load interconnection request outcomes, such as withdrawal rates, success rates, and processing times.

This information will allow for (i) more accurate transmission system and large load planning and development, and (ii) regulators and market participants to make more informed decisions regarding resource adequacy, load growth, and reliability.
